output:
# MONOCHROME and PRISM: Curing the Reverse-KL Diversity Tax in Text-to-Image Preference Alignment with an Arbitrary-*f*-Divergence, Reward-Free, RL-Free Trajectory Surrogate that Provably Recovers Diffusion-DPO

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Modern text-to-image (T2I) generators are diffusion models — DDPM [1], Stable Diffusion / latent diffusion [2], and SDXL [3] — trained by denoising score matching to approximate the data log-likelihood. That objective is agnostic to the qualities humans actually care about (prompt faithfulness, aesthetics, absence of artifacts), so the field imports the reinforcement-learning-from-human-feedback (RLHF) recipe from language modeling: collect pairwise human preferences, fit a reward model under the Bradley–Terry assumption (e.g., PickScore [13], ImageReward [15], HPS v2 [14]), and fine-tune the generator to maximize that reward while a **reverse-KL penalty** to the pretrained reference model regularizes the update and curbs reward hacking. DDPO [4] casts denoising as a multi-step Markov decision process and applies policy gradients; DPOK [5] fine-tunes T2I diffusion online with an explicit KL regularizer. These methods work but inherit RLHF's pathologies: a separately trained, gameable reward model, on-policy sampling cost, and brittle, high-variance optimization.

Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [6] removed the explicit reward model and RL loop from LLM alignment by observing that the KL-regularized reward-maximization problem has a closed-form optimal policy; substituting it into the Bradley–Terry likelihood yields a simple classification loss whose implicit reward is the log-ratio between policy and reference. Diffusion-DPO [7] (Wallace et al., CVPR 2024) ports this to T2I diffusion: because the diffusion likelihood is intractable, it bounds the objective with a per-timestep evidence-lower-bound (ELBO) surrogate over the denoising trajectory, training directly on preference pairs such as Pick-a-Pic [13]. D3PO [8] (Yang et al., CVPR 2024) reaches a related reward-model-free objective by treating the denoising chain as an MDP and arguing the policy is its own optimal reward model. A fast-growing follow-up family refines credit assignment and sampling — SPO [9] supplies a step-aware preference signal aligned with denoising performance at each timestep, and variants such as SePPO, NCPPO, and DNO explore semi-/noise-conditioned and direct-noise objectives. **Every one of these methods is built on the reverse-KL-regularized optimal policy** — the divergence is hard-wired, never a design variable.

In *language*-model alignment, the field has already recognized that reverse-KL is just one point in a space of admissible regularizers. The "Beyond Reverse KL" line, *f*-DPO [10] (Wang et al., ICLR 2024), generalizes DPO to an **arbitrary *f*-divergence** constraint, deriving the implicit-reward link from the inverse of `f′` / the convex conjugate of `f`, and showing that *mass-covering* divergences (forward KL, JS, α-divergences) better preserve generation **diversity** than mode-seeking reverse-KL while retaining alignment. χPO [11] (Huang et al., ICLR 2025) takes a complementary, robustness-driven view: a one-line change replacing the log link with a χ²-divergence objective provably curbs the **over-optimization** that KL-regularization is too weak to prevent. Together these works establish, *for text*, that the divergence is a first-class knob with concrete diversity and robustness consequences. The corresponding generalization for *diffusion / T2I* — where the likelihood is intractable and the objective must be defined over a denoising trajectory — has **not** been worked out.

Why the divergence choice matters is classical, from variational inference and the *f*-GAN program [12], which showed any *f*-divergence can be optimized variationally via its convex conjugate. Minimizing **reverse** KL is *mode-seeking* (zero-forcing): it concentrates mass on a few high-reward modes and is content to drop the rest — exactly what one wants for sharp alignment, but also a recipe for collapse. **Forward** KL and the other mass-covering members of the *f*-family (JS, and the α-divergence interpolation that smoothly connects forward and reverse KL) instead penalize dropping reference modes, trading a little peak alignment for retained spread. Preference fine-tuning under reverse-KL therefore sits at one extreme of a principled spectrum, and the *f*-divergence view turns the diversity–alignment trade-off into an explicit, tunable axis rather than an accident of the loss.

That this is not merely theoretical is now well documented. In LLMs, Kirk et al. [20] (ICLR 2024) show RLHF improves out-of-distribution reward but *measurably reduces output diversity* relative to supervised fine-tuning — a generalization-vs-diversity tension. For images, practitioners report the homogenized, "samey" aesthetic of heavily preference-tuned T2I models: outputs converge to a narrow stylistic mode, losing per-prompt variety and rare concepts. Yet the T2I alignment literature [7,8,9] is still evaluated almost exclusively on alignment/win-rate metrics, with diversity an afterthought, even though mature diversity instruments exist — the Vendi score [16], improved precision/recall [17], density/coverage [18], and perceptual-similarity measures such as DreamSim [19].

**The gap is now explicit.** Reverse-KL is structurally mode-seeking; the *f*-divergence cure is established for text but absent for diffusion; the intractable-likelihood obstacle remains unaddressed under a general divergence; and diversity is under-measured in T2I preference tuning. Stated sharply: the entire T2I alignment field optimizes under a **fixed reverse-KL constraint and has no principled, tractable way to move off it** — that reverse-KL → arbitrary-*f*-divergence gap is the wedge this work closes.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

**The phenomenon — MONOCHROME collapse (the reverse-KL diversity tax).** We name the structural side-effect of reverse-KL preference tuning **MONOCHROME collapse**: a *Mode-cONcentrating* erosion of per-prompt variety in which a preference-aligned T2I model funnels every prompt toward a narrow band of compositions, palettes, and styles — the homogenized "RLHF look." The mechanism is precise and not a tuning artifact. Reverse-KL, `KL(π‖π_ref)`, is *zero-forcing*: its optimum is the exponential tilt `π*(x_0|c) = (1/Z(c))·π_ref(x_0|c)·exp(r(x_0|c)/β)`, which places mass on the few highest-reward modes and is structurally content to drop others. So the very property that makes reverse-KL good for sharp alignment makes it pay a **diversity tax**, mirroring the *measured* diversity loss documented for RLHF'd LLMs [20]. MONOCHROME collapse is largely invisible to pooled metrics: FID and PickScore are computed over a *pool* of prompts, so a model can collapse every prompt to a single mode and still report healthy pooled FID, because the pool still spans many prompts. Detecting it requires **per-prompt** diversity measurement (many samples for one prompt) with instruments like Vendi [16], precision/recall [17], density/coverage [18], LPIPS, and DreamSim [19].

**The method — PRISM.** We introduce **PRISM** (*Preference alignment via f-divergence Regularization for Image diffuSion Models*) — a prism preserves the full spectrum, i.e., diversity. PRISM starts from the standard regularized RLHF objective but replaces reverse-KL with a **general *f*-divergence**: `max_π E_{x_0~π}[r(x_0|c)] − β·D_f(π(·|c)‖π_ref(·|c))`. Its core mechanism is four interlocking pieces:

- **The `(f′)⁻¹` stationarity optimum.** Adding the normalization constraint with multiplier `λ(c)` and setting the pointwise Lagrangian derivative to zero gives `π*(x_0|c)/π_ref(x_0|c) = (f′)⁻¹((r(x_0|c) − λ(c))/β)`. For `f(u)=u log u` (reverse-KL), `(f′)⁻¹(y)=exp(y−1)` recovers the Boltzmann tilt that DPO inverts — so the clean log-linear reward↔log-ratio link is *special to reverse-KL*. For general `f` the link is a divergence-specific nonlinearity.
- **The divergence-specific link `h_f = f′`.** Inverting the optimum yields the implicit reward `r(x_0|c) = β·f′(π*/π_ref) + λ(c)`. Defining `h_f(·) := f′(·)`, PRISM is "logistic ∘ `h_f`." Whereas naive DPO composes the logistic with `log(π/π_ref)`, PRISM composes it with `h_f`, which is the *only* place the chosen divergence enters the loss.
- **Partition-function cancellation in Bradley–Terry.** Plugging the implicit reward into `P(x^w ≻ x^l|c) = σ(r(x^w|c) − r(x^l|c))`, the prompt-dependent `λ(c)` **cancels in the win−lose difference** (exactly as `log Z` cancels in vanilla DPO), giving a partition-function-free pairwise loss `L_PRISM = −E log σ(β·[f′(u_θ^w) − f′(u_θ^l)] − β·[f′(u_ref^w) − f′(u_ref^l)])` with `u = π_θ/π_ref`. For reverse-KL, `h_f(u)=log u + 1` and the `+1` constants cancel, so `L_PRISM` collapses **exactly** to the Diffusion-DPO loss.
- **A trajectory-level *f*-divergence surrogate that defeats diffusion's intractable likelihood.** `π_θ(x_0|c)` is an intractable marginal. PRISM defines the divergence on the **joint trajectory** `D_f^{traj}(π(x_{0:T}|c)‖π_ref(x_{0:T}|c))`, estimates the per-timestep log density ratio `g_t = log p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t,c) − log p_ref(x_{t-1}|x_t,c)` (closed-form for Gaussian reverse kernels as a scaled MSE difference, exactly as Diffusion-DPO), forms an ELBO-style trajectory estimate `ĝ = Σ_t w_t g_t`, sets `û_θ = exp(ĝ)`, and feeds `h_f(û_θ)=f′(û_θ)` into the loss. **Honest caveat:** because `f′` is nonlinear in the log-ratio, Jensen does *not* give a free upper bound for arbitrary `f`. PRISM therefore *proves* the surrogate is a valid bound only for the **curvature-bounded subfamily** (α ∈ [0,1], JS), where `f′` is suitably concave/Lipschitz on the operating range; for the remaining divergences it treats `ĝ` as a **consistent, empirically validated estimator**, not a bound — and states this assumption openly.

**The novelty wedge (C1–C4).** PRISM is the first method to deliver, jointly: **C1** — a closed-form, reward-model-free *and* RL-free preference loss for diffusion valid under an **arbitrary *f*-divergence** (link via `(f′)⁻¹` / the convex conjugate); **C2** — a **trajectory-level *f*-divergence surrogate** for diffusion's intractable likelihood (not just reverse-KL); **C3** — an **explicit diversity–alignment trade-off objective and evaluation**, with per-prompt diversity treated as a first-class target/metric; **C4** — an **α-divergence interpolation that provably recovers reverse-KL Diffusion-DPO** as a special case.

**The α-divergence knob.** Sweeping `α ∈ [0,1]` traces a continuous path from mode-seeking to mass-covering: `α→1` → **reverse-KL (recovers Diffusion-DPO)**, the special-case anchor; `α→0` → **forward-KL** (mass-covering); `α=0.5` → near-Hellinger/JS behavior; `α=2` → χ². This single dial operationalizes the diversity–alignment trade-off (C3) and unifies a fragmented method zoo into points on one continuum (C4).

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can text-to-image diffusion preference alignment be generalized from its hard-wired reverse-KL constraint to an arbitrary *f*-divergence — through a reward-model-free, RL-free, trajectory-level surrogate that defeats the intractable diffusion likelihood — so that a single α-divergence knob explicitly controls the diversity–alignment trade-off and provably recovers Diffusion-DPO at the reverse-KL limit?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

**P1 — Deployment ubiquity raises the stakes of how we align T2I models.** Diffusion generators [1,2,3] are among the most widely deployed generative systems in the world, and preference fine-tuning [7,8,9] is now the default last-mile step before release. A *structural* side-effect of that step (mode-seeking MONOCHROME collapse) propagates to an enormous user base, so a principled fix has outsized practical reach.

**P2 — Diversity collapse is a real, reported failure of preference-tuned image models.** The homogenized "RLHF look" — models converging to a narrow band of compositions, palettes, and styles — mirrors the *measured* diversity loss documented for RLHF'd LLMs [20]. Reverse-KL's zero-forcing is the mechanistic culprit, so addressing the divergence directly targets the root cause rather than patching symptoms.

**P3 — Representational coverage, creativity, and fairness are at risk.** When a model drops modes it preferentially discards rare concepts, minority styles, and long-tail demographic or cultural depictions, narrowing both creative range and representational fairness. A method that lets practitioners dial in mass-covering behavior (forward-KL / α / JS regimes) is a concrete lever to retain that coverage where it matters.

**P4 — A principled, tunable divergence knob unifies a fragmented alignment literature.** Diffusion-DPO, D3PO, SPO, and reward-model RLHF [4,5,7,8,9] are today distinct recipes that all silently fix reverse-KL. Exposing the *f*-divergence as a single design axis — with `α→1` recovering Diffusion-DPO as a *provable* special case (C4) — turns these into points on one continuum, giving practitioners an interpretable trade-off dial instead of a method zoo.

**P5 — Rigorous diversity evaluation becomes first-class, not an afterthought.** Pairing the objective with mature diversity instruments — Vendi score [16], improved precision/recall [17], density/coverage [18], DreamSim-based perceptual spread [19] — alongside alignment metrics (PickScore [13], ImageReward [15], HPS v2 [14]) establishes a reusable benchmark protocol for the whole T2I alignment field, which currently under-reports diversity.

**P6 — Cross-modal and theoretical ripple effects.** The construction connects three established threads — *f*-GAN's variational *f*-divergence estimation [12], the mode-seeking/mass-covering theory of variational inference, and *f*-divergence RLHF in LLMs [10,11] — and transfers them into the diffusion setting. The trajectory-level *f*-divergence surrogate (C2) is itself a reusable tool that could generalize beyond preference tuning to distillation, guidance, and other diffusion fine-tuning objectives, amplifying the contribution's impact.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

**O1 — [BINDING] The DPO reparameterization is reverse-KL-specific (representational).** Vanilla DPO works because reverse-KL's optimum is an exponential tilt, making the implicit reward *linear in the log density ratio* — the clean logistic link. For a general `f`, the optimum is `(f′)⁻¹((r−λ)/β)` and the implicit reward is `β·f′(π/π_ref)`, a **nonlinear** link `h_f`. **Why naive fails:** if you merely swap the divergence in the regularizer but keep the `log(π/π_ref)` loss, you silently still optimize reverse-KL — you change β, not the divergence; the divergence only enters through `h_f=f′`, so without composing the logistic with `h_f` there is *no behavioral change*. **Type: representational.** This is the single binding obstacle the whole method is organized around: if `h_f` cannot be made tractable, PRISM collapses back to Diffusion-DPO.

**O2 — Diffusion's intractable likelihood forces a per-divergence surrogate with a fragile inequality direction (optimization + representational).** Diffusion-DPO's ELBO+Jensen bound is clean *only* for reverse-KL, because `f′=log` is linear in the log-ratio so the trajectory expectation passes through. For general `f`, `f′` is nonlinear and `E[f′(ĝ)] ≠ f′(E[ĝ])`; Jensen does **not** give a free upper bound unless `f′` has the right curvature on the operating range. **Why naive fails:** plugging the ELBO log-ratio estimate into an arbitrary `h_f` can yield a surrogate that is neither an upper bound nor a consistent estimator → you minimize the wrong objective. **Type: optimization + representational.** Mitigation: prove the bound for the curvature-bounded subfamily (α ∈ [0,1], JS) and treat the rest as validated estimators. This is the binding *implementation* consequence of O1 and the honest soft spot of contribution (b).

**O3 — Estimator variance blow-up for mass-covering divergences (optimization).** Forward-KL and α→0 put weight `f′(u)=−1/u` where `u=π_θ/π_ref` is small — exactly where `π_ref` is tiny but `π_θ` is not — so `f′(û_θ)` and any importance weights **explode**, giving high-variance, heavy-tailed gradients (the classic forward-KL/importance-weight pathology). **Why naive fails:** unclipped training diverges or oscillates for precisely the divergences we most want (the diverse ones). **Type: optimization.** Mitigation: ratio/importance-weight clipping, self-normalization, and operating-range clamps on `û_θ`.

**O4 — Cross-divergence comparison requires matched *effective* regularization (evaluation).** β couples with the curvature of `f` at `u=1` (`f″(1)` sets the local penalty strength), so a fixed β imposes *different* effective regularization across reverse-KL, JS, and χ². **Why naive fails:** comparing divergences at equal β compares β, not the divergence — a measured diversity/alignment difference could just be "more regularization." **Type: evaluation.** Mitigation: rescale β per divergence so `β·f″(1)` (or an empirically matched held-out KL-to-ref) is constant across runs, and report the matched quantity.

**O5 — Diversity is hard to measure and attribute; pooled metrics are blind to per-prompt collapse (evaluation).** FID/PickScore are computed over a *pool* of prompts, so a model can collapse every prompt to a single mode yet keep good pooled FID. "Good" diversity (semantic coverage of valid interpretations) and "bad" diversity (artifacts) are also conflated. **Why naive fails:** reporting only FID/PickScore cannot detect or attribute MONOCHROME collapse — the exact phenomenon the paper is about. **Type: evaluation.** Mitigation: **per-prompt** diversity (N samples / same prompt) via pairwise LPIPS & DreamSim [19], Vendi [16], plus precision/recall [17] and density/coverage [18] against a reference set, with quality controlled so diversity gains aren't just noise.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The wedge needs four clauses *jointly*: **C1** arbitrary-*f* reward-free + RL-free diffusion loss; **C2** trajectory-level *f*-divergence surrogate for the intractable likelihood; **C3** explicit diversity–alignment objective + measurement; **C4** provable α-recovery of reverse-KL Diffusion-DPO. No prior line covers more than two.

**Near-miss 1 — Diffusion-DPO (Wallace et al., CVPR 2024) [7].** *Covers:* C1 partially (reward-free, RL-free closed-form diffusion preference loss) and C2 partially (the per-timestep ELBO surrogate over the trajectory — but specialized to reverse-KL). *Misses:* the general-*f* form of C1/C2 (loss hard-wired to reverse-KL); C3 (no diversity objective or measurement); C4 (it *is* the reverse-KL point but provides no knob to move off it). It is the very special case PRISM's C4 must recover.

**Near-miss 2 — D3PO (Yang et al., CVPR 2024) [8].** *Covers:* C1 partially (reward-model-free, MDP-based direct preference fine-tuning of diffusion). *Misses:* general-*f* C1/C2 (reverse-KL/Bradley–Terry implicit-reward only); C3 (alignment/safety-distortion focus, no diversity target); C4 (no divergence interpolation).

**Near-miss 3 — SPO (Liang et al., CVPR 2025) [9].** *Covers:* refines *where* the preference signal applies (step-aware credit assignment along the trajectory) — orthogonal to, and composable with, PRISM. *Misses:* all four clauses as stated — still reverse-KL-based (no general *f*, C1/C2), no explicit diversity trade-off (C3), no α-recovery (C4). It improves *step granularity*, not the *divergence family*.

**Near-miss 4 — Reward-model RLHF for diffusion: DDPO [4] / DPOK [5].** *Covers:* on-policy KL-regularized fine-tuning of diffusion (the pre-DPO paradigm). *Misses:* C1 entirely (requires a separately trained reward model and an RL loop — neither reward-free nor RL-free); KL term is reverse-KL only (no general-*f* C2); C3 (no diversity objective); C4 (no recovery framing).

**Near-miss 5 — *f*-DPO "Beyond Reverse KL" (Wang et al., ICLR 2024) [10]** *(closest conceptual prior)*. *Covers:* the general-*f* implicit-reward derivation and the diversity motivation — but for **LLMs / tractable-likelihood autoregressive policies**. It supplies the convex-conjugate machinery behind C1 and the diversity intuition behind C3, and includes α-/JS members relevant to C4. *Misses:* C2 entirely — it never confronts diffusion's **intractable likelihood**; there is no denoising-trajectory *f*-divergence surrogate. It does not instantiate the method for T2I diffusion, provides no image-diversity protocol (C3 for vision), and proves no Diffusion-DPO recovery (C4). PRISM is, in effect, the diffusion-trajectory realization of this idea — the hard, unsolved part.

**Near-miss 6 — χPO / χ²-Preference Optimization (Huang et al., ICLR 2025) [11].** *Covers:* a specific non-KL (χ²) link with provable robustness to over-optimization — evidence that the divergence choice carries formal consequences. *Misses:* C1/C2 for diffusion (LLM-only, single fixed χ² link, not an arbitrary-*f* family with a trajectory surrogate); C3 (robustness framing, not a diversity–alignment trade-off); C4 (no α-interpolation recovering reverse-KL Diffusion-DPO).

**Crisp novelty summary.** PRISM is the first reward-model-free, RL-free preference-alignment method for T2I diffusion under an **arbitrary *f*-divergence**, realized through a **trajectory-level *f*-divergence surrogate** that defeats the intractable-likelihood obstacle, with an **α-divergence knob that provably recovers reverse-KL Diffusion-DPO** and explicitly optimizes (and measures) the diversity–alignment trade-off.

**Joint coverage gaps no prior work fills together:** (1) no existing diffusion alignment method (Diffusion-DPO [7], D3PO [8], SPO [9], DDPO [4], DPOK [5]) admits a general-*f* divergence — all are reverse-KL; (2) the general-*f* DPO theory ([10], [11]) exists *only* for tractable-likelihood LLMs, so the trajectory-level surrogate for diffusion (C2) is unaddressed anywhere; (3) no T2I preference method optimizes diversity as a first-class objective with a divergence knob (C3+C4 together); (4) the provable reverse-KL/Diffusion-DPO recovery via α-interpolation (C4) is, to our knowledge, unstated in any prior work. (SePPO, NCPPO, and DNO appear in the follow-up narrative but are still reverse-KL-based and do not close these gaps.)

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

**Q5a — METHOD.** Base models: **Stable Diffusion 1.5** (fast iteration, ablations) and **SDXL 1.0** (headline results), matching Diffusion-DPO [7]. Loss: PRISM (`L_PRISM`) instantiated for **{reverse-KL, forward-KL, JS, α-divergence (sweep α ∈ {0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1}), χ²}**, with reverse-KL (α=1) the special-case anchor. Training: LoRA fine-tune of the UNet (rank 8–64) for the full sweep to keep compute tractable, plus one full-UNet fine-tune per top divergence to confirm LoRA doesn't distort conclusions; AdamW, lr ~1e-5 (full) / 1e-4 (LoRA), ~128–256 preference pairs per batch (gradient-accumulated), bf16, EMA, ~2k–4k steps over Pick-a-Pic v2. Matched regularization: β chosen per divergence so `β·f″(1)` (and an empirically measured held-out KL-to-ref) is held constant across divergences (O4). Stability: density-ratio/importance-weight clipping and `û_θ` operating-range clamps for mass-covering runs (O3); trajectory-surrogate-vs-naive-marginal toggle.

**Q5b — DATASETS.** Training: **Pick-a-Pic v2** [13] (~1M human preference pairs; ~851k used for SDXL), win/lose pairs `(c, x^w, x^l)`. Evaluation prompt sets: Pick-a-Pic v2 **test** split, **PartiPrompts (P2)** (1.6k prompts, broad categories), **HPS v2** [14] benchmark prompts, and **MS-COCO 2014 30k** captions (for FID/CLIP fidelity).

**Q5c — METRICS.** Alignment: **PickScore** [13], **HPS v2** [14], **ImageReward** [15], LAION aesthetic predictor, and pairwise win-rate vs Diffusion-DPO (human eval on a subset + VLM judge for scale). Per-prompt diversity (the headline axis): for each eval prompt generate **N=16–32 samples** (fixed sampler, varied seeds), then compute (i) mean pairwise **LPIPS** and **DreamSim** [19] within the N samples, (ii) **Vendi score** [16] over the N-sample embedding set, (iii) **improved precision/recall** [17] and **density/coverage** [18] vs a per-prompt reference bank; also report CFG/noise-scale sweeps to trace diversity–quality curves. Fidelity: **FID** (vs COCO 30k) and **CLIPScore**. Trade-off summary: alignment (PickScore/HPS) vs per-prompt diversity (Vendi/recall) → **Pareto front**.

**Q5d — BASELINES & ABLATIONS.** Baselines: SFT/base (no alignment), **Diffusion-DPO (reverse-KL = special-case anchor)** [7], **D3PO** [8], **SPO** [9], and a reward-model RLHF anchor (**DDPO** [4] / **DPOK** [5]). Ablations: (1) divergence type (core result); (2) β at matched effective regularization (isolates divergence geometry from strength — O4); (3) α-interpolation curve tracing the diversity–alignment Pareto front (the knob, C4); (4) weight-clipping on/off (O3); (5) trajectory surrogate vs naive marginal (validates C2/O2); (6) LoRA vs full UNet (robustness).

**Q5e — VALIDATION / FALSIFIABILITY.** (1) **Exact special-case check:** PRISM at α=1 reproduces Diffusion-DPO within training noise on PickScore/FID/diversity — *falsifies the derivation if it doesn't.* (2) **Mass-covering dominance:** forward-KL/JS/α≤0.5 Pareto-dominate Diffusion-DPO — strictly higher per-prompt diversity (Vendi/recall/LPIPS) at *equal* alignment (matched PickScore) — *falsifies the core claim if no divergence beats the reverse-KL point.* (3) **Monotone frontier:** sweeping α 0→1 traces a monotone diversity↓ / alignment↑ frontier — *falsifies the "knob" claim if the curve is non-monotone or collapses.*

**Q5f — EXPECTED OUTCOMES (hypotheses with rough magnitudes).** **H1:** Reverse-KL (Diffusion-DPO) gives best raw alignment but worst per-prompt diversity — quantifying the MONOCHROME premise. **H2:** Forward-KL / JS / α≈0.25–0.5 retain materially higher diversity at equal alignment — e.g. **+15–35% mean pairwise LPIPS** and **+0.1–0.3 normalized Vendi / +0.05–0.15 recall** at matched PickScore (Δ within ±0.3% of Diffusion-DPO). **H3:** Gains are largest on open-ended/ambiguous prompts (PartiPrompts abstract & world-knowledge) and smallest on highly specified prompts. **H4:** χ² over-penalizes and is unstable without clipping → ablation shows clipping is necessary for χ²/forward-KL (supports O3). **H5:** Trajectory-surrogate beats naive marginal in both stability and final Pareto position (supports C2/O2).

**Q5g — COMPUTE / FEASIBILITY.** LoRA on SD1.5 for the full divergence × β × α grid: each run ~few GPU-hours on a single A100/H100; whole sweep feasible on 4–8 A100/H100 in days. SDXL headline runs (LoRA + a few full-UNet): each ~1–3 GPU-days on 4–8 A100s. Eval cost is dominated by N×prompts sampling (per-prompt diversity needs 16–32 samples × thousands of prompts × many models) — budget batched inference; this, not training, is the real cost driver. Total: a small-academic-cluster-scale project, kept tractable by LoRA.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] J. Ho, A. Jain, P. Abbeel, "Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models," NeurIPS, 2020.
[2] R. Rombach, A. Blattmann, D. Lorenz, P. Esser, B. Ommer, "High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models," CVPR, 2022.
[3] D. Podell, Z. English, K. Lacey, A. Blattmann, T. Dockhorn, J. Müller, J. Penna, R. Rombach, "SDXL: Improving Latent Diffusion Models for High-Resolution Image Synthesis," arXiv:2307.01952, 2023.
[4] K. Black, M. Janner, Y. Du, I. Kostrikov, S. Levine, "Training Diffusion Models with Reinforcement Learning" (DDPO), ICLR, 2024.
[5] Y. Fan, O. Watkins, Y. Du, H. Liu, M. Ryu, C. Boutilier, P. Abbeel, M. Ghavamzadeh, K. Lee, K. Lee, "DPOK: Reinforcement Learning for Fine-tuning Text-to-Image Diffusion Models," NeurIPS, 2023.
[6] R. Rafailov, A. Sharma, E. Mitchell, C. D. Manning, S. Ermon, C. Finn, "Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model," NeurIPS, 2023.
